description: This figure presents the engineering stress-strain curves of low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with three different carbon contents: 0.5 wt% C (black curve, labeled 0.5C), 0.8 wt% C (red curve, labeled 0.8C), and 1.2 wt% C (green curve, labeled 1.2C). The x-axis represents engineering strain, while the y-axis represents engineering stress in units of MPa. As observed, as carbon content increases from 0.5 wt% to 1.2 wt%, the total elongation of the steels rises continuously, reaching a maximum of 56.2% for the 1.2C sample. The ultimate tensile strength first increases to a peak value of 979 MPa for the 0.8C sample, then slightly reduces to 947 MPa for the 1.2C sample. The 1.2C steel shows the most excellent strength-ductility combination, with its strength-ductility product reaching 53.2 GPa%, nearly twice that of the 0.5C steel, verifying the remarkable influence of carbon content on the tensile performance of this low-density steel system.
